title: Magic Weapons
description: Blades, bludgeons, and instruments of war with something extra — not just enhancement bonuses but history, character, opinion, and the accumulated weight of everything they've been through. The weapon as an artifact with a past.

entries.result: A shortsword that stays warm to the touch regardless of environment, running slightly warmer when held by someone with a fever, cold blood, or recent dishonesty.
A warhammer with a head engraved with a mountain range. When struck against stone, it leaves the impression of a specific peak that doesn't match any known mountain.
A longbow whose bowstring never needs replacing, but that grows a new ring on its grip each year, and currently has forty-seven.
A dagger that hums at a frequency just below hearing when drawn near magical objects, audible only as a pressure sensation in the wielder's teeth.
A greataxe that cannot be sheathed in the same scabbard twice — each time it must be a new one, or held bare.
A rapier that becomes invisibly sharp — cuts made with it don't bleed for four seconds, which is longer than it sounds in a fight.
A spear whose shaft is carved from a single piece of heartwood from a tree that no longer exists, which has made it the subject of academic interest it does not welcome.
A crossbow that loads itself at walking pace, drawing new bolts from the quiver without the wielder's hands, but only when the wielder is moving.
A mace with a head carved in the image of a face that visitors describe differently every time — some see serenity, some see fury, some see grief.
A hand axe that, when thrown, returns — not quickly, not dramatically, but always, sometimes hours later, in the wielder's pack or by their bedroll.
A bastard sword in a scabbard that resizes to any wearer. The sword itself does not resize. The scabbard is more remarkable than the sword.
A flail whose chain has never rusted and can't be cut, despite showing the wear of extensive use, with links that occasionally rearrange themselves slightly.
A knife that was used for a specific political assassination and that has never been cleaned. It cannot be cleaned — whatever tries to remove the stain is absorbed.
A longbow whose arrows always travel in silence, regardless of fletching or target, the impact completely silent even when the damage is not.
A greatsword that is heavier than physics explains — normal in grip, impossible when set down, requiring the wielder's will to move it.
A sling whose stones always find the hand that loads them, returning to the wielder's grip over the course of a day regardless of distance.
A sword with a name engraved on the blade in a dead language that translates to 'Not Yet' — and that has never, in its recorded history, dealt a killing blow.
A battleaxe claimed by three different noble families as their ancestral heirloom, with three different family seals engraved on three different surfaces.
A morningstar that produces a brief flash when it makes solid contact — not fire, not lightning, something older than either — visible only to the wielder.
A spear whose point is made from a material no smith has identified, that cannot be sharpened because it never dulls.
A short sword that drains warmth from the air when drawn in cold climates, and heat in warm ones, maintaining a temperature the wielder finds comfortable.
A two-handed hammer that resonates at the frequency of compacted earth when swung, so that striking stone produces a low tone and striking packed soil produces a high one.
A dagger that belonged to a famous killer, then to the investigator who caught the killer, then to the executioner, and has a reputation that precedes it into a room.
A glaive whose blade extends and retracts by the wielder's thought, from the length of a dagger to the reach of a spear, the extra length emerging from the air itself.
A bow that draws memories from the wielder's mind with each arrow nocked, the most recent memory going first, returned when the arrow is recovered.
A sword that becomes longer in proportion to the number of people watching the fight — barely a shortsword in private, nearly a greatsword before an audience.
A club made from the thighbone of a giant, unadorned, unenchanted by any wizard, but treated as a sacred object by every giant it is shown to.
A rapier that leaves marks on whatever it touches — not cuts, marks, like a signature — that fade over a week but are legible to those who know what to look for.
A war hammer whose head is composed of compressed historical time — every strike delivers the impact of thousands of uses rather than one.
A blade forged in a volcano that still hasn't fully cooled, glowing faintly at its core, and that will need to be returned to volcanic heat once a century or it will go dark.
A sword that refuses to harm certain animals — specifically, certain animals, a list that doesn't map onto any known taxonomy or magical tradition.
A dagger that tastes iron when drawn near something that intends harm to its wielder, the taste intensifying with proximity and certainty of intent.
A mace belonging to a saint whose canonization is disputed, which some religious authorities consider a holy relic and others consider a theft from a legitimate estate.
A sword that was used to kill a god — not permanently, but successfully, for a day — and has never been the same about it.
A hand crossbow that loads with thoughts instead of bolts — the wielder imagines the bolt and it appears, at the cost of a vivid memory, expended on the shot.
A spear whose tip has been to the bottom of the ocean and returned. It is wet on its tip, always, regardless of conditions.
A scimitar that was the instrument of a famous mercy — used to free a thousand slaves in a single campaign — and is now associated with liberation in the culture it came from.
A warhammer that weighs exactly what the wielder expects it to weigh, which is not always the same number.
A sword that hums when lie-speakers hold it, the hum increasing with the magnitude of the untruth, useless in combat, invaluable in negotiation.
A shortsword from a culture that no longer exists, made by techniques that haven't been reproduced, recognized by scholars as irreplaceable and by everyone else as a very good knife.
A lance that always tilts toward the correct target in a joust — not to help the wielder win, but to indicate which opponent it considers worthy.
A sword that is half-sheathed in a block of stone it was never pulled from, and that was used in this configuration as a weapon by its last owner, who was creative.
A dagger that was buried with its owner, dug up, buried with the next, dug up, and is now on its seventh burial cycle, currently exhumed.

A bow that fires only when aimed at something the wielder genuinely intends to hit, and that resists — physically, the string won't draw — when aimed in anger or malice at something the wielder loves.
A sword that shows the wielder what it's seen — visions, brief, overlapping — when held in specific temperatures, showing the climates it has fought in.
A warhammer with a head that's hollow and full of something that shifts when moved, audible, unidentified, that has been inside since manufacture.
A blade with a counterweight that doesn't exist — the balance is perfect regardless of the angle, as if something invisible holds the tip up.
A javelin that returns always to the hand that threw it, and that has been doing this for a century and a half, outlasting every owner who has tried to give it away.
A dagger that is perfectly balanced to be thrown left-handed, which is how it was made, and which improves the abilities of left-handed wielders while subtly hampering the right-handed.
A sword with a blade that is black in daylight and bright in darkness, each environment reversing its visibility in a way that has tactical implications.
A hunting knife that never needs sharpening and that always finds the grain in wood, stone, and occasionally argument.
A sword with a sentience that isn't loud about it — it doesn't speak, doesn't broadcast emotion — but that occasionally moves in the grip in a way that expresses preference.
A greatbow made for a giant, functional for a smaller archer if they brace it, and capable of ranges that have no relevant defensive architecture.
A pair of short swords made to be used together, separated decades ago, whose owners have been compelled toward each other ever since without realizing why.
A sword that is technically cursed — the curse makes the wielder incapable of using any other weapon — but that is good enough that most wielders stop minding.
A battle axe that was used to cut down a sacred tree and that carries guilt for it — not in the blade but in the wielder, transferred on contact.
A saber presented to a general at the end of a war, engraved with the names of the battles it was present for, the last name misspelled.
A crossbow enchanted to never miss, provided the wielder believes the shot will connect — the enchantment is psychological, not magical, which is either profound or useless depending on the wielder.
A sword that takes on the characteristics of whatever it kills — absorbing the last property of each creature slain — and has now accumulated a collection that defies description.
A knife made from the tooth of a dragon, which the dragon is aware of, and which the dragon is waiting for its return before it will consider the matter settled.
A spear with a shaft that has been replaced six times and a head that has never been replaced and that is the oldest object the party is likely to hold.
A sword that cannot be drawn against the person who named it, which was tradition — the naming was a binding — and which is now complicated because the namer is dead.
A sword that speaks, once, to each new wielder — a single sentence, never repeated, always accurate, always uncomfortable — and is then silent.
A war hammer used to destroy a temple, then used to build another, and that carries the conflicted weight of both events in equal measure.
A blade that was forged to kill a specific person and that failed — the person is still alive — and that has been looking for an opportunity to correct this for forty years.
A throwing knife that can change direction once in flight, at the wielder's thought, which has made it notorious as a weapon of unfairness in dueling culture.
A sword that grows lighter the more desperate the wielder's situation, as if designed for the moment when the wielder has nothing left to lose.
A bow that was used in every major battle of a kingdom's history — whether this is literally true or ceremonially maintained, the bow considers itself a veteran.
A glaive decorated with the heraldry of a noble family whose line ended, and that has been waiting to resume employment with a successor it considers worthy.
A sword that makes no sound — no ring, no whistle, no impact sound — in absolute silence, which is more unnerving in practice than in theory.
A mace that was used exclusively for executions and that has no tradition of use in combat, which does not make it less effective in combat, only unhappy about it.
A hand axe that was used to sever a contract — literally, the signed document — in a ceremony that bound the magic to the act, and that now severs obligations on contact.
A sword that was stolen, given back, stolen again, given back again, and is currently being given back for the third time, and both parties involved are tired of the cycle.
A spear with a head that shifts between materials — iron, bronze, obsidian, bone — on no apparent schedule, each material sharing properties with the source.
A sword that can be used to open any lock, given enough time, which is not why it was made, and which the lockpicking community is divided on the ethics of.
A knife that was used in a famous peace treaty — to cut the bread, not to threaten — and that was somehow the most important object in the room.
A shortsword that vibrates at the frequency of the wielder's heartbeat, in their hand, noticeable in the quiet before a fight, useful for calibration.

A sword that has broken five times and been reforged five times, each reforging making it slightly different, and that is now the kind of thing that is what it is because of everything that happened to it.
A hatchet that was carried by a sailor and that consistently points to the nearest water source, which was very useful at sea and is not useless on land.
A blade given as a gift by a god who regrets the giving and has been trying to get it back through intermediaries who have all been unsuccessful.
A sword that has a name in four languages and that responds differently to being called by each one — more eager in one language, more reluctant in another.
A crossbow that predates the invention of the crossbow, demonstrably, and that raises more questions than it resolves.
A sword that belongs to a hero's descendant by rights of inheritance that the descendant has never known about and whose claim is legitimate.
A blade that leaves wounds that cannot be healed by magic — only time — which is not a curse so much as a design philosophy.
A war hammer that strikes with the full weight of everything its previous wielders believed in, which is heavier than iron and lighter than nothing.
A sword whose blade shows, faintly, the reflections of places it has been rather than where it currently is.
A knife carved from the wood of a gallows tree, functional as a blade, used historically by executioners who considered it lucky, and still considered such by their successors.
A lance that was jousted with by a champion and that remembers the wins but not the losses, which has given it an inflated opinion of everyone it has been couched for.
A sword that produces a specific smell — pine resin, old leather — when drawn, that the previous wielder associated with home.
A shortsword that was the last thing a famous poet made before switching exclusively to writing, who forged it himself and was apparently better at it than at poetry.
A blade that must be fed — not blood, something stranger — at the dark of each moon, or it grows dull, and the feeding has become a ritual the current wielder isn't sure about.
A sword that was the prize in a duel between two rivals who have both since died, leaving the sword without the context that made it meaningful and it feeling the loss.
A knife that has been used only for kindness — cutting rope to free people, cutting chains, cutting binding threads — and that is reluctant to do anything else.
A sword that functions perfectly in the hands of someone who has never used a sword before and that grows increasingly difficult for experts to wield.
A spear that was thrown from a mountain and landed here, which was three hundred years ago, and that no one has been able to throw since, though many have tried.
A sword belonging to a living person who does not know it is here, who will be very interested to learn this, and whose response to that information is difficult to predict.
A blade that is currently at peace with what it is — past, present, purpose — which is unusual in enchanted weapons and slightly alarming to scholars who know what usually makes them that way.
A sword that was presented at a coronation, used in a war, broken in a betrayal, reforged in a reconciliation, and has now outlasted the dynasty it served by two centuries.
A blade whose name, if spoken, compels a specific ancient being to look up from whatever it's doing — a connection established centuries ago, still active, whose other end is still occupied.

A sword that was made to end a specific thing — a specific evil, a specific era, a specific possibility — and that has been waiting since its forging, and that the current wielder has just walked into the vicinity of what it was made for.
